terms.def: Mean-reverting stochastic process: dX = κ(θ−X)dt + σdW; κ = speed of reversion, θ = long-run mean.
ln(2)/κ — expected time for the spread to revert halfway to the mean; key metric for strategy sizing.
Augmented Dickey-Fuller test — tests for a unit root; rejection means the series is stationary (mean-reverting).
H < 0.5 = mean-reverting; H = 0.5 = random walk; H > 0.5 = trending. Measures degree of long-range dependence.
Two non-stationary series form a stationary spread; the basis for pairs trading and relative value strategies.
(Spread − mean) / std; signal used to enter/exit mean-reversion trades when the spread is sufficiently extreme.

eli3: Mean reversion is the tendency for asset prices or spreads to return toward their long-run average after moving away from it. If something is abnormally high, it tends to come back down; if abnormally low, it tends to recover. This is the foundation of value investing (cheap stocks revert toward fair value) and many quantitative strategies.

Mean reversion operates at multiple timescales: seconds (market microstructure — bid-ask bounce); days to weeks (stat arb, pairs trading); months to years (value factor, contrarian investing); decades (CAPE/Shiller P/E mean reversion in equity markets). Different strategies exploit different timescales with different holding periods and turnover.

The key tension: mean reversion vs. momentum. Both exist in financial data — momentum at short-to-medium horizons (1-12 months), mean reversion at very short (< 1 week) and very long (3-5 years) horizons. A quant strategy must correctly identify which regime it's in before trading.

whenWhy.title: Testing for mean reversion: stationarity and half-life
whenWhy.body: A time series X_t is mean-reverting if it's stationary (returns to its mean over time). Tests: (1) ADF test (Augmented Dickey-Fuller): tests H0: unit root (random walk, not mean-reverting) vs. H1: stationary. Reject H0 at p < 0.05 to confirm mean reversion. (2) Hurst exponent H: H < 0.5 = mean-reverting; H = 0.5 = random walk; H > 0.5 = trending. Estimated via variance ratio or rescaled range analysis. (3) Variance ratio test (Lo & MacKinlay): compare variance of k-period returns to k × variance of 1-period returns; ratio < 1 indicates negative autocorrelation (mean reversion). Half-life of mean reversion: fit AR(1): X_t = α + φ X_{t-1} + ε. Half-life = -log(2)/log(φ). For equity prices directly: unit root almost always; for spreads, residuals, or log price ratios: often stationary.

whenWhy.title: Mean reversion in volatility
whenWhy.body: Volatility is strongly mean-reverting — this is a foundational assumption of GARCH and stochastic volatility models. After a volatility spike (e.g., a market crash), implied volatility (VIX) tends to revert toward its long-run mean. VIX mean level: ~20; spikes to 40-80 in crises (2008: 80, 2020: 66, 2011: 48). After spikes, VIX reverts to ~15-20 within 3-6 months historically. Strategies: (1) Short VIX futures after volatility spikes (volatility carry trade); (2) Sell short-dated options and buy longer-dated options (term structure plays). Risk: volatility mean reversion can be slow; a position short VIX after a spike can suffer further losses before reversion. Extreme events (2008) can break historical mean-reversion assumptions temporarily.

whenWhy.title: Cross-sectional mean reversion: contrarian equity strategies
whenWhy.body: DeBondt & Thaler (1985): stocks with extreme losses over 3-5 years ('losers') subsequently outperform stocks with extreme gains ('winners'). This is long-horizon cross-sectional mean reversion — the value factor at its core. Short-horizon (1-week): strong short-term reversal — last week's losers tend to be this week's winners, especially in small-caps. This is a microstructure effect (price impact of order flow reverting). Contrarian strategy: buy last month's worst performers, short last month's best performers. Works best with: (1) small-cap, liquid universe; (2) daily rebalancing; (3) industry-neutral construction to avoid stale sector bets. Signal decays rapidly — requires careful transaction cost management.

deepDiveIntro: The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process is the standard continuous-time model for mean reversion. It has three parameters: a mean reversion speed that determines how quickly the process is pulled back, a long-run equilibrium level it reverts to, and a volatility parameter. In discrete time it becomes a first-order autoregressive process, so the mean reversion speed can be estimated by regressing today's value on yesterday's value. The half-life — how long it takes for a deviation to shrink by half — is derived directly from the autoregressive coefficient and determines the appropriate trading frequency for a mean-reversion strategy.

deepDive.heading: Variance Ratio Tests and Non-Linear Mean Reversion
deepDive.body: The variance ratio test compares long-horizon return variance to the variance scaled by the number of periods. Under a random walk the ratio equals one; a ratio below one signals mean reversion at that horizon, while a ratio above one signals momentum. For the S&P 500, the ratio follows a U-shaped pattern: it sits below one at weekly horizons (short-term reversal), rises above one for one-to-twelve-month horizons (momentum), and falls below one again at three-to-five-year horizons (long-run mean reversion). Non-linear mean reversion means the reversion speed increases with the size of the deviation, suggesting larger positions when deviations are larger. [1]
deepDive.formula: VR(k) = Var(r_{t,t+k}) / (k · Var(r_t)),    VR < 1 ⇒ mean reversion

deepDive.heading: Mean Reversion in Interest Rates and the Vasicek Model
deepDive.body: Short-term interest rates exhibit strong mean reversion because monetary policy typically forces rate spikes back toward equilibrium. The Vasicek model describes this with a long-run mean level and a speed of adjustment, producing a stationary distribution of rates. Using historical Fed Funds data over a seventy-year span, the speed parameter implies a half-life of one to three years. When short rates are far above the estimated neutral rate, mean reversion suggests they will decline; an inverted yield curve similarly implies eventual steepening. [2]
deepDive.formula: dr_t = κ(θ − r_t) dt + σ dW_t,    half-life = ln(2)/κ

deepDive.heading: Limits to Arbitrage: Why Mean Reversion Isn't Free Money
deepDive.body: If assets reliably mean-revert, why don't arbitrageurs drive profits to zero? Limits to arbitrage (Shleifer & Vishny 1997): (1) Noise trader risk: mispriced assets can become more mispriced before reverting; a mean-reversion strategy suffers mark-to-market losses that may trigger margin calls before the position profits; (2) Synchronisation risk: even if all arbitrageurs know an asset is mispriced, any individual arbitrageur doesn't know when others will trade — they may trade early and lose to subsequent divergence; (3) Horizon mismatch: mean reversion at 3-year horizon requires 3-year capital commitment; most institutional investors face redemption risk (clients can withdraw capital if short-term performance is poor). These limits explain why mean reversion persists — it's not free arbitrage; it requires capital, patience, and tolerance for interim losses. [2]

takeaways: Test for mean reversion: ADF test (unit root), Hurst exponent H < 0.5, or variance ratio test VR(k) < 1. [1]
OU process: dX = κ(μ-X)dt + σdW; half-life = log(2)/κ; larger κ = faster reversion. [1]
Mean reversion exists at multiple horizons: < 1 week (microstructure), 3-5 years (value/contrarian). [1]
Limits to arbitrage: noise trader risk, margin calls, and horizon mismatch prevent easy exploitation. [2]
Volatility is strongly mean-reverting: VIX spikes to 40-80 in crises, reverts to ~15-20 within months. [1]

edgeCases.q: When does mean reversion become dangerous in practice?
edgeCases.a: Mean reversion strategies fail catastrophically when: (1) the underlying relationship breaks permanently (structural break): e.g., a pair trade between an oil major and a refiner breaks when the oil major vertically integrates; (2) the mean itself shifts: e.g., interest rate mean in Japan has been near zero for 30 years — models calibrated to US rate history will persistently predict reversion to wrong equilibrium; (3) the strategy is too crowded: simultaneous unwinding by correlated strategies amplifies losses before reversion. The LTCM crisis (1998) is the canonical example: convergence trades on sovereign bond spreads that had always previously converged failed when spreads widened further during the Russia default/LTCM unwind, forcing margin-call liquidations at maximum loss.

edgeCases.q: How do you estimate the equilibrium mean when it's non-stationary?
edgeCases.a: Cointegration: if X_t and Y_t are individually non-stationary (unit root) but their linear combination X_t - β Y_t is stationary, use the cointegrating relationship as the mean. The 'mean' is time-varying but moves slowly. Kalman filter with a random walk state: model the equilibrium μ_t as a random walk; X_t = μ_t + ε_t, μ_t = μ_{t-1} + η_t. The Kalman filter updates the mean estimate as new data arrives. This handles both regime shifts and non-stationary means adaptively. For spread trading: the Kalman filter hedge ratio adapts as the fundamental relationship between the two assets changes slowly — more robust than fixed OLS estimates.

sources.label: Lo, A.W. & MacKinlay, A.C. (1988) — Stock Market Prices Do Not Follow Random Walks, RFS
Shleifer, A. & Vishny, R.W. (1997) — The Limits of Arbitrage, JF
